title: Topological Triplons in the Pinwheel Valence Bond Solid on the Kagome Lattice
authors: Laura Calonge-Martínez, Peng Rao, Frédéric Mila, Johannes Knolle
abstract: We investigate the triplon excitations of the pinwheel valence-bond-solid phase on the deformed kagome lattice compound Rb2Cu3SnF12. Using bond-operator mean-field theory, we compute the triplon band structure, dynamical structure factor, Berry curvatures and the associated thermal Hall response. We show that the presence of Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions and an external magnetic field are key for endowing triplon bands with nontrivial Chern numbers. We find good qualitative agreement of the low-energy dynamical structure factor with neutron-scattering experiments. An applied magnetic field can isolate the lowest triplon Chern band leading to a tunable thermal Hall conductivity for accessible temperature and field regimes. Our results establish the deformed kagome pinwheel valence-bond solid as a promising platform for topological triplon physics and for observing the associated thermal Hall effect.
summary: The research analyzes the exotic triplon excitations within a pinwheel Valence Bond Solid on a kagome lattice. By incorporating Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions and an external magnetic field, the authors predict that the system can host non-trivial band topology characterized by Chern numbers. This topological feature is directly linked to a measurable, tunable thermal Hall effect.
main_problem: The study investigates topological triplon excitations and their associated thermal Hall response in a specific quantum magnet system.
main_result: The deformed kagome pinwheel VBS is shown to be a promising platform for observing topological triplon physics, leading to a tunable thermal Hall conductivity when DMI and an external magnetic field are present.
method: Calculations employ bond-operator mean-field theory combined with the Bogoliubov–de Gennes formalism to determine band structures and topological invariants like Chern numbers.
model_or_system: The system is the compound Rb2Cu3SnF12, which realizes a pinwheel Valence Bond Solid (VBS) on a deformed kagome lattice. The Hamiltonian includes Heisenberg exchange, Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions, and an external magnetic field.
key_observables: Triplon band structure, dynamical structure factor S(q, w), Berry curvatures, Chern numbers, and thermal Hall conductivity (kappa_xy).
important_parameters: DMI parameter d_z = 0.18; exchange couplings J1=18.6 meV, J2=0.95J1, J3=0.85J1, J4=0.55J1.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mean-field approximations and neglects higher-order interaction terms (e.g., triplon–triplon interactions) in the structure factor calculations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the lattice structure, schematic bond operator description, band splittings under DMI/B field, agreement of S(q, w) with neutron scattering data, and dependence of kappa_xy on T and B.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the VBS ground state using bond operators to calculating spectral properties (S(q,w)) by comparing theory to experiment. It then focuses on topological analysis via Berry curvature and culminates in deriving a finite thermal Hall conductivity formula based on Chern numbers.
why_interesting: This work applies advanced many-body theoretical tools (bond operators, BdG formalism) to predict measurable transport phenomena ($\kappa_{xy}$) arising from emergent topological order in frustrated quantum magnets.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Deformed kagome lattice structure of Rb2Cu3SnF12. The four inequivalent nearest-neighbor exchange couplings J1–J4 are indicated by distinct bond types, forming a 12-site pinwheel VBS unit cell. Red bonds denote singlet dimers, while arrows indicate the orientation of the out-of-plane DMI Dz ij. (b) Schematic summary of the bond operator description.

FIG. 2. Triplon energy bands for the nearest-neighbor pinwheel valence-bond solid on the kagome lattice. (a) Spectrum in the absence of DMI and magnetic field, showing threefold degenerate triplet modes. (b) Inclusion of DMI with dz = 0.18. (c) Addition of a small magnetic field further splits the transverse (x and y) triplon branches. (d) Larger magnetic field leading to a clear isolation of the lowest-energy band.

FIG. 3. Static structure factor S (q) = S xx(q) + S yy(q) + S zz(q) for dz = 0.18 and zero magnetic field, obtained by assembling 64 reduced Brillouin zones. (a) The two panels correspond to the two chiral configurations. (b) S (q) averaged over both configurations. The background grid indicates the tiling of the the 64 shifted reduced Brillouin zones used in the construction, while the hexagon highlighted in yellow marks the first Brillouin zone

FIG. 4. Dynamical structure factor S (q, ω) = S xx(q, ω) + S yy(q, ω) + S zz(q, ω) at zero temperature. (a) Intensity along a one-dimensional momentum path (indicated in green in panel (c)) for dz = 0.18 and zero magnetic field. (b) Same as in (a) in the presence of an external magnetic field of intensity h = 1.5 meV applied along the z direction, showing the Zeeman splitting of the triplon branches. (c) dynamical structure factor intensity integrated over the low-energy window 0.4 ≤ω ≤1.0 with no magnetic field, plotted in an extended Brillouin zone scheme obtained by assembling 64 zones. The results are averaged over the two pinwheel domains.
FIG. 5. Transverse thermal Hall conductivity κxy as a function of temperature for several values of applied magnetic field in the z- direction.
